text.title: Terraform State Operations
text.summary: State commands reference, import and moved blocks, force-replace, unlock procedures, and remote state data sources.

section.title: Import Blocks (Terraform 1.5+)

rule.statement: Use import blocks in .tf files instead of terraform import CLI commands for all new import work; import blocks are reviewable, version-controlled, and run as part of terraform plan.
rule.rationale: CLI import runs immediately with no plan preview. Import blocks show the planned import in terraform plan output, allowing review before apply. They are also idempotent — already-imported resources are ignored.

section.note: After writing import blocks, run: terraform plan -generate-config-out=generated.tf to auto-generate the resource configuration for imported resources.

rule.statement: Use moved blocks (Terraform 1.1+) to rename resources or move them to modules without touching the state directly; keep moved blocks in the configuration until all users have applied.
rule.rationale: moved blocks express refactoring declaratively in code. They are visible in plan output, can be reviewed, and avoid the error-prone manual state mv commands.

section.title: State Commands

rule.statement: Before any manual state operation, backup state with terraform state pull > backup.tfstate; verify the backup; communicate with the team to prevent concurrent modifications.
rule.rationale: Manual state operations are irreversible without a backup. Remote backends have locking but do not protect against logical errors in state commands.

section.note: Key state commands: terraform state list (view all resources), terraform state show addr (inspect), terraform state mv src dst (rename), terraform state rm addr (unmanage), terraform state pull/push (download/upload).

rule.statement: After any state operation, run terraform plan to verify the state reflects reality with no unexpected changes or diffs.
rule.rationale: State commands can leave gaps between state and actual infrastructure. A clean plan (exit code 0) confirms consistency.

rule.statement: Use terraform apply -replace=resource.name to force replacement of a specific resource; never use terraform taint (deprecated since 0.15.2).
rule.rationale: -replace is explicit, requires an apply confirmation, and shows the replacement in plan output. taint modifies state without a plan preview.

rule.statement: Use terraform force-unlock LOCK_ID only after confirming no other apply is running; diagnose the lock cause before unlocking — a crash leaves a stale lock, not an active process.
rule.rationale: Unlocking while an apply is running allows two concurrent applies, which corrupts state. force-unlock is a recovery tool, not a workaround for slow pipelines.

antipattern.description: Using terraform state push to overwrite remote state without a backup or plan verification.
antipattern.reason: Push overwrites the remote state immediately. If the pushed state is incorrect, there is no automatic rollback — you must recover from versioning.
